objectives.text: Set sampler primitive options such as resilience levels
Bypass runtime error mitigations and implement your own
Understand the theoretical background behind the sampler primitive

questions.stem: You have transpiled a circuit for your backend, producing the ISA circuit `isa_qc`, and created `sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)`. Which call correctly submits the circuit for execution?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

# isa_qc: circuit already transpiled for `backend`
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
job = ...  # which call?

options.text: `job = sampler.run([isa_qc])`
`job = sampler.run(isa_qc)`
`job = sampler.run([isa_qc], backend)`
`job = sampler.run([isa_qc], backend=backend)`

explanation.correct: SamplerV2.run() takes an iterable of PUBs (Primitive Unified Blocs). A bare ISA circuit is a valid PUB-like element, but it must be wrapped in a list: `sampler.run([isa_qc])`. The backend is not passed to run() — it was already bound via `mode=` when the primitive was constructed.

distractors.B: V1 primitives accepted bare circuits, but SamplerV2.run() requires an iterable of PUBs — passing the circuit unwrapped raises an error.
distractors.C: run() has no positional backend parameter — execution target is fixed by `mode=` at construction time; this raises a TypeError.
distractors.D: There is no `backend=` keyword on run() — that's a V1-migration misconception; the mode is set on the primitive itself.

evidence.A: call succeeded on fake_manila: returned job, result()[0].data.meas gave counts over 4 bitstrings, 1024 shots total
evidence.B: call raised ValueError: An invalid Sampler pub-like was given (<class 'qiskit._accelerate.circuit.CircuitInstruction'>). If you want to run a single circuit, you need to wrap it with `
evidence.C: call raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
evidence.D: call raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() got an unexpected keyword argument 'backend'

questions.stem: A Bell circuit is measured and submitted with `sampler.run([isa], shots=1000)`. What is the value of `sum(result[0].data.meas.get_counts().values())`?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

# isa: transpiled Bell circuit with measure_all()
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
result = sampler.run([isa], shots=1000).result()
counts = result[0].data.meas.get_counts()
print(sum(counts.values()))

options.text: `1000` — the counts across all outcomes always add up to the number of shots
`2` — a Bell state only produces the outcomes `00` and `11`
`500` — the shots are split evenly between the two measured qubits
`1024` — the sampler always uses its 1024 default shots

explanation.correct: get_counts() tallies every shot into its observed bitstring, so the counts sum to the total number of shots that were run. Here shots=1000 was passed explicitly, so the tallies sum to 1000 regardless of how many distinct bitstrings (2, 3, or 4) actually appear.

distractors.B: This confuses the number of *distinct outcomes* with the number of shots. get_counts() returns a dict whose keys are the distinct bitstrings but whose values sum to the shot count.
distractors.C: Shots are not divided among qubits — every shot measures the full register once, producing one bitstring; all 1000 bitstrings are tallied.
distractors.D: 1024 is only the default when no shots are supplied. Here `shots=1000` overrides the default, so exactly 1000 shots run.

evidence.A: option claims sum(counts.values())=1000; executed run gave 1000 -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims sum(counts.values())=2; executed run gave 1000 -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims sum(counts.values())=500; executed run gave 1000 -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims sum(counts.values())=1024; executed run gave 1000 -> match=False

questions.stem: After running a SamplerV2 job on a circuit that used `measure_all()`, you access `bits = result[0].data.meas`. Which statement about `bits` is correct?

options.text: `bits` is a `BitArray` holding the per-shot measurement outcomes; call `bits.get_counts()` to summarize them into a dict
`bits` is already a dict that maps each bitstring to its count
`bits` is a NumPy array of measurement probabilities, one per basis state
`bits` is a `Counts` object, exactly like `result.get_counts()` returned in Qiskit V1

explanation.correct: SamplerV2 stores each register's outcomes in a BitArray inside the DataBin (`data.meas`). A BitArray keeps the raw per-shot bitstrings; you turn it into a summary histogram with `get_counts()` (dict) or read shots directly with `get_bitstrings()`. It is not itself the counts dict.

distractors.B: The counts dict is what `bits.get_counts()` *returns*; `bits` itself is the richer BitArray container of per-shot data, not the dict.
distractors.C: The Sampler returns samples, not a probability vector. Frequencies must be estimated by counting shots yourself; nothing in `data.meas` is a probability array.
distractors.D: This is a V1-migration misconception. V2 has no top-level `result.get_counts()`; results live in per-register BitArrays reached through `data.<register>`.

evidence.A: claim holds=True — type(bits).__name__='BitArray', isinstance(bits,dict)=False, get_counts() is dict=True
evidence.C: claim holds=False — type name is 'BitArray', not ndarray
evidence.D: claim holds=False — type name is 'BitArray', not Counts

questions.stem: You build `sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)` and never touch its options, then call `sampler.run([isa])` with no `shots` argument. How many shots does the job run?
questions.code:
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
job = sampler.run([isa])   # no shots given, options untouched
num = job.result()[0].data.meas.num_shots

options.text: 1024 — the default value used when neither `shots` nor `default_shots` is set
4096 — the SamplerV2 default
1 — one shot unless you ask for more
It raises an error, because `shots` is a required argument

explanation.correct: When you pass neither a per-run `shots` value nor set `options.default_shots`, SamplerV2 falls back to its built-in default of 1024 shots. `shots` is optional precisely because this default exists.

distractors.B: 4096 is not the Sampler's default; the built-in fallback is 1024. This is a memorized-wrong-number distractor.
distractors.C: A single shot is never the default — sampling needs many repetitions to estimate a distribution. The fallback is 1024.
distractors.D: `shots` is optional. Omitting it is legal and simply uses the default (1024), so no error is raised.

questions.stem: This code raises an error when it reaches `sampler.run(...)`. What is the correct fix?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.h(0); qc.cx(0, 1); qc.ccx(0, 1, 2)
qc.measure_all()

sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)   # backend is a real/fake device
job = sampler.run([qc])             # <-- raises here

options.text: Transpile the circuit to an ISA circuit for the backend first (e.g. with `generate_preset_pass_manager`), then run the ISA circuit
Wrap the circuit in one more list: `sampler.run([[qc]])`
Just pass a shots count: `sampler.run([qc], shots=1024)`
Construct the sampler with no mode: `SamplerV2().run([qc])`

explanation.correct: Runtime primitives only accept ISA circuits — circuits whose gates and connectivity already match the backend's target. The raw circuit here uses gates (like the Toffoli) that are not in the backend basis, so `run` raises an input-validation error. Transpiling with a preset pass manager for the backend produces the ISA circuit that runs successfully.

distractors.B: The problem is not the list nesting — a single list of PUBs is already correct. Nesting changes nothing about the circuit still being non-ISA, so it still fails.
distractors.C: Adding `shots` does not make a non-ISA circuit acceptable; the validation error is about gates/connectivity, not the shot count.
distractors.D: Dropping the mode removes the execution target entirely and does not transpile anything; the circuit is still non-ISA and construction/run fails.

evidence.A: transpile->ISA then run: SUCCESS, counts sum=128
evidence.B: still raised IBMInputValueError: 'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target
evidence.C: still raised IBMInputValueError: 'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target
evidence.D: raised ValueError: A backend or session must be specified.

questions.stem: Hoping to turn on error mitigation for measurement counts, a developer writes `sampler.options.resilience_level = 1` on a `SamplerV2`. What actually happens?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2

sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
sampler.options.resilience_level = 1

options.text: It raises an error — `SamplerV2` has no `resilience_level`; that option belongs to `EstimatorV2`
It enables T-REx readout error mitigation on the sampled counts
It is accepted but silently ignored, because the sampler has no mitigation
It is an alias that turns on measurement twirling

explanation.correct: `resilience_level` is an EstimatorV2 option — resilience levels drive expectation-value mitigation such as TREX/ZNE. SamplerV2 exposes no `resilience_level`; assigning it raises a validation error ('no such attribute'). The Sampler's error-handling options are `dynamical_decoupling`, `twirling`, and execution/simulator settings — it returns raw samples and leaves mitigation to you.

distractors.B: Readout mitigation on expectation values (TREX) is reached through Estimator resilience settings, not through a Sampler option — the Sampler has none.
distractors.C: It is not silently ignored: the options object validates attributes and raises on an unknown one, so the line throws immediately.
distractors.D: There is no aliasing. Measurement twirling is set explicitly via `options.twirling.enable_measure`; `resilience_level` simply does not exist on the Sampler.

evidence.A:
setting resilience_level raised=True (ValidationError: 1 validation error for SamplerOptions
resilience_level
  Object has no attribute 'resilience_level' [type=no_such_attribute, input_value=1, ); hasattr=False

questions.stem: A 3-qubit circuit applies X to qubit 0 only, then measures qubit i into classical bit i. In the Sampler's counts, which bitstring dominates?
questions.code:
qc = QuantumCircuit(3, 3)
qc.x(0)                       # only qubit 0 is flipped
qc.measure(0, 0)
qc.measure(1, 1)
qc.measure(2, 2)
# ... transpile, then sampler.run([isa]) ...
counts = result[0].data.c.get_counts()

options.text: `'001'` — Qiskit prints bitstrings little-endian, so classical bit 0 (qubit 0) is the rightmost character
`'100'` — qubit 0 is the leftmost character in the bitstring
`'111'` — flipping any qubit flips the whole register
`'000'` — X on qubit 0 has no effect on the measured bits

explanation.correct: Qiskit uses little-endian bit ordering: in the printed bitstring the classical bit with the highest index is on the left and bit 0 is on the right. Only qubit 0 is excited, and it is measured into classical bit 0, so the rightmost character is 1 and the rest are 0 -> '001'.

distractors.B: This assumes big-endian ordering (qubit 0 leftmost). Qiskit is little-endian, so qubit 0 lands in the rightmost position, giving '001', not '100'.
distractors.C: X acts only on the qubit it targets; qubits 1 and 2 stay in |0>. The register does not collectively flip.
distractors.D: X on qubit 0 flips it to |1>, so its measured bit is 1, not 0 — the outcome cannot be all zeros.

evidence.A: option claims dominant bitstring '001'; observed argmax='001' (counts['001']=1706) -> match=True
evidence.B: option claims dominant bitstring '100'; observed argmax='001' (counts['100']=11) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims dominant bitstring '111'; observed argmax='001' (counts['111']=0) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims dominant bitstring '000'; observed argmax='001' (counts['000']=111) -> match=False
